items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: What is the difference between aerobic and muscular endurance?
items.correct: Aerobic endurance is the cardiovascular system's ability to supply oxygen; muscular endurance is a muscle's ability to contract repeatedly without fatigue.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same component.
items.random_wrong: Aerobic is for legs, muscular for arms.
items.why: Students treat both as 'being able to keep going'. Aerobic endurance is WHOLE-BODY cardio capacity; muscular endurance is LOCALISED to specific muscles (e.g., sit-up reps).

items.q: A cartwheel takes place in which plane and axis?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Frontal plane, frontal axis. “Sagittal plane, transverse axis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which lever class typically gives mechanical advantage?
items.why: Correct answer: 2nd class. “1st class” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If effort arm is 60 cm and load arm is 20 cm, what is the mechanical advantage?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
